For more information, see individual Prussian state articles (links in above History section). 2. **[^](#ref_b)** The position of **Ministerpräsident** was introduced in 1792 when Prussia was a Kingdom; the Minister-Presidents shown here are the heads of the Prussian republic. | |

([/ˈprʌʃə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Preußen* [[ˈpʁɔʏsn̩]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Preu%C3%9Fen.ogg "File:De-Preußen.ogg"); [Old Prussian](/wiki/Old_Prussian "Old Prussian"): *Prūsija*) was a [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans") state centred on the [North European Plain](/wiki/North_European_Plain "North European Plain"). It originated from the 1525 [secularization](/wiki/Secularization "Secularization") act of the [Prussian](/wiki/Prussia_(region) "Prussia (region)") part of the [State of the Teutonic Order](/wiki/State_of_the_Teutonic_Order "State of the Teutonic Order"). For centuries, the [House of Hohenzollern](/wiki/House_of_Hohenzollern "House of Hohenzollern") ruled Prussia, expanding its size with the [Prussian Army](/wiki/Prussian_Army "Prussian Army"). Prussia, with its capital at [Königsberg](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsberg "Königsberg") and then, when it became the [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") in 1701, [Berlin](/wiki/History_of_Berlin "History of Berlin"), decisively shaped the [history of Germany](/wiki/History_of_Germany "History of Germany"). Prussia formed the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") when it united the German states in 1871. It was *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* dissolved by [an emergency decree](/wiki/1932_Prussian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1932 Prussian coup d'état") transferring powers of the Prussian government to [German Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany") [Franz von Papen](/wiki/Franz_von_Papen "Franz von Papen") in 1932 and *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* by [an Allied decree](/wiki/Abolition_of_Prussia "Abolition of Prussia") in 1947.

**Prussia** ([/ˈprʌʃə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Preußen* [[ˈpʁɔʏsn̩]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Preu%C3%9Fen.ogg "File:De-Preußen.ogg"); [Old Prussian](/wiki/Old_Prussian "Old Prussian"): *Prūsija*) was a [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans") state centred on the [North European Plain](/wiki/North_European_Plain "North European Plain"). It originated from the 1525 [secularization](/wiki/Secularization "Secularization") act of the [Prussian](/wiki/Prussia_(region) "Prussia (region)") part of the [State of the Teutonic Order](/wiki/State_of_the_Teutonic_Order "State of the Teutonic Order"). For centuries, the [House of Hohenzollern](/wiki/House_of_Hohenzollern "House of Hohenzollern") ruled Prussia, expanding its size with the [Prussian Army](/wiki/Prussian_Army "Prussian Army"). Prussia, with its capital at [Königsberg](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsberg "Königsberg") and then, when it became the [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") in 1701, [Berlin](/wiki/History_of_Berlin "History of Berlin"), decisively shaped the [history of Germany](/wiki/History_of_Germany "History of Germany"). Prussia formed the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") when it united the German states in 1871. It was *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* dissolved by [an emergency decree](/wiki/1932_Prussian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1932 Prussian coup d'état") transferring powers of the Prussian government to [German Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany") [Franz von Papen](/wiki/Franz_von_Papen "Franz von Papen") in 1932 and *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* by [an Allied decree](/wiki/Abolition_of_Prussia "Abolition of Prussia") in 1947.

The name *Prussia* derives from the [Old Prussians](/wiki/Old_Prussians "Old Prussians") who were conquered by the [Teutonic Knights](/wiki/Teutonic_Knights "Teutonic Knights") – an organized [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") medieval [military order](/wiki/Military_order_(religious_society) "Military order (religious society)") of [German crusaders](/wiki/Prussian_Crusade "Prussian Crusade") – in the 13th century. In 1308, the Teutonic Knights conquered the region of [Pomerelia](/wiki/Pomerelia "Pomerelia") with [Danzig](/wiki/Danzig "Danzig"). Their [monastic state](/wiki/State_of_the_Teutonic_Order "State of the Teutonic Order") was mostly [Germanised](/wiki/Germanisation "Germanisation") through [immigration from central and western Germany](/wiki/Ostsiedlung "Ostsiedlung"), and, in the south, it was [Polonised](/wiki/Polonisation "Polonisation") by settlers from [Masovia](/wiki/Masovia "Masovia"). The imposed [Second Peace of Thorn (1466)](/wiki/Second_Peace_of_Thorn_(1466) "Second Peace of Thorn (1466)") split Prussia into the western [Royal Prussia](/wiki/Royal_Prussia "Royal Prussia"), a province of Poland, and the eastern [Duchy of Prussia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Prussia "Duchy of Prussia"), a feudal fief of the [Crown of Poland](/wiki/Crown_of_Poland "Crown of Poland") until 1657. After 1525, the [Teutonic Order](/wiki/Teutonic_Order "Teutonic Order") relocated their headquarters to [Mergentheim](/wiki/Mergentheim "Mergentheim"), but managed to keep land in [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia "Livonia") until 1561. The [union of Brandenburg and the Duchy of Prussia](/wiki/Brandenburg-Prussia "Brandenburg-Prussia") in 1618 led to the proclamation of the [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") in 1701.

Prussia entered the ranks of the [great powers](/wiki/Great_power "Great power") shortly after becoming a kingdom.[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5) It became increasingly large and powerful in the 18th and 19th centuries. It had a major voice in European affairs under the reign of [Frederick the Great](/wiki/Frederick_the_Great "Frederick the Great") (1740–1786). At the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") (1814–15), which redrew the map of Europe following Napoleon's defeat, Prussia acquired rich new territories, including the coal-rich [Ruhr](/wiki/Ruhr "Ruhr"). The country then grew rapidly in influence economically and politically, and became the core of the [North German Confederation](/wiki/North_German_Confederation "North German Confederation") in 1867, and then of the German Empire in 1871. The Kingdom of Prussia was now so large and so dominant in the new Germany that *[Junkers](/wiki/Junker_(Prussia) "Junker (Prussia)")* and other Prussian elites identified more and more as [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans") and less as Prussians.

The Kingdom ended in 1918 along with other German monarchies that were terminated by the [German Revolution](/wiki/German_Revolution "German Revolution"). In the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic"), the [Free State of Prussia](/wiki/Free_State_of_Prussia "Free State of Prussia") lost nearly all of its legal and political importance following the [1932 coup](/wiki/1932_Prussian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1932 Prussian coup d'état") led by [Franz von Papen](/wiki/Franz_von_Papen "Franz von Papen"). Subsequently, it was effectively dismantled into Nazi German *[Gaue](/wiki/Gau_(territory) "Gau (territory)")* in 1935. Nevertheless, some Prussian ministries were kept and [Hermann Göring](/wiki/Hermann_G%C3%B6ring "Hermann Göring") remained in his role as [Minister President of Prussia](/wiki/Minister_President_of_Prussia "Minister President of Prussia") until the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). [Former eastern territories of Germany](/wiki/Former_eastern_territories_of_Germany "Former eastern territories of Germany") that made up a significant part of Prussia lost the majority of their German population after 1945 as the [Polish People's Republic](/wiki/Polish_People%27s_Republic "Polish People's Republic") and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") both absorbed these territories and had most of its German inhabitants [expelled](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans "Flight and expulsion of Germans") by 1950. Prussia, deemed "a bearer of militarism and reaction" by the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"), was officially abolished by an Allied declaration in 1947. The international status of the former eastern territories of the Kingdom of Prussia was disputed until the [Treaty on the Final Settlement with Respect to Germany](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Final_Settlement_with_Respect_to_Germany "Treaty on the Final Settlement with Respect to Germany") in 1990, but its return to Germany remains a cause among [far-right](/wiki/Far-right "Far-right") politicians, the [Federation of Expellees](/wiki/Federation_of_Expellees "Federation of Expellees") and various political [revanchists](/wiki/Revanchists "Revanchists") and [irredentists](/wiki/Irredentists "Irredentists").

The terms "Prussian" and "[Prussianism](/wiki/Prussianism "Prussianism")" have often been used, especially outside Germany, to denote the militarism, military professionalism, aggressiveness, and conservatism of the *Junker* class of landed aristocrats in the East who dominated first Prussia and then the German Empire.

The central emblems of Prussia were the [black eagle](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Prussia "Coat of arms of Prussia") and the [flag of Prussia](/wiki/Flag_of_Prussia "Flag of Prussia"), which placed the eagle on a white field. Black and white had long-standing associations in the region. They were used by the [Teutonic Knights](/wiki/Teutonic_Knights "Teutonic Knights") and later by the [Hohenzollern dynasty](/wiki/Hohenzollern_dynasty "Hohenzollern dynasty"). The Teutonic Order’s white surcoat, [embroidered](/wiki/Embroidery "Embroidery") with a black cross edged in gold and bearing the black imperial eagle, established the colour pairing that became closely tied to Prussian identity.

Black and white were eventually combined with the red-and-white colours of the [Hanseatic](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") cities—[Bremen](/wiki/Bremen "Bremen"), [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") and the [Lübeck](/wiki/Free_City_of_L%C3%BCbeck "Free City of Lübeck")—and with the colours of [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg"). This produced the black–white–red commercial flag of the [North German Confederation](/wiki/North_German_Confederation "North German Confederation"), adopted later as the flag of the German Empire in 1871.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Latin motto *[Suum cuique](/wiki/Suum_cuique "Suum cuique")* (‘to each, his own’) originated with the [Order of the Black Eagle](/wiki/Order_of_the_Black_Eagle "Order of the Black Eagle"), founded by King [Frederick I](/wiki/Frederick_I_of_Prussia "Frederick I of Prussia") in 1701, and came to be widely associated with Prussia. The [Iron Cross](/wiki/Iron_Cross "Iron Cross"), introduced in 1813 by King [Frederick William III](/wiki/Frederick_William_III_of_Prussia "Frederick William III of Prussia"), also became a prominent Prussian symbol.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The region, first settled by Baltic [Old Prussians](/wiki/Old_Prussians "Old Prussians") who were later Christianised, became a destination for migrants over several centuries, including (and later mainly) Protestant [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans") as well as [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") and [Lithuanians](/wiki/Lithuanians "Lithuanians") living near the borderlands. (*See *[Ostsiedlung](/wiki/Ostsiedlung "Ostsiedlung")*.*)

## Territory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Territory")]

Before its abolition, the territory of the [Free State of Prussia](/wiki/Free_State_of_Prussia "Free State of Prussia") included the provinces of [East Prussia](/wiki/East_Prussia_Province "East Prussia Province"); [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg_Province "Brandenburg Province"); [Saxony](/wiki/Province_of_Saxony "Province of Saxony") (including much of the present-day state of [Saxony-Anhalt](/wiki/Saxony-Anhalt "Saxony-Anhalt") and parts of the state of [Thuringia](/wiki/Thuringia "Thuringia") in Germany); [Pomerania](/wiki/Province_of_Pomerania_(1815%E2%80%931945) "Province of Pomerania (1815–1945)"); [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland_Province "Rhineland Province"); [Westphalia](/wiki/Westphalia_Province "Westphalia Province"); [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia_Province "Silesia Province") (without [Austrian Silesia](/wiki/Czech_Silesia "Czech Silesia")); [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Province_of_Schleswig-Holstein "Province of Schleswig-Holstein"); [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover_Province "Hanover Province"); [Hesse-Nassau](/wiki/Hesse-Nassau_Province "Hesse-Nassau Province"); and a small detached area in the south called [Hohenzollern](/wiki/Hohenzollern_Province "Hohenzollern Province"), the ancestral home of the Prussian ruling family. The land that the Teutonic Knights occupied was flat and covered with fertile soil. The area was perfectly suited to the large-scale raising of wheat.[[5]](#cite_note-6) The rise of early Prussia was based on the raising and selling of wheat. Teutonic Prussia became known as the "bread basket of Western Europe" (in German, *Kornkammer*, or granary). The port cities which rose on the back of this wheat production included: Stettin in Pomerania (now [Szczecin](/wiki/Szczecin "Szczecin"), Poland); Danzig in Prussia (now [Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk"), Poland); [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga") in Livonia (now Riga, Latvia); [Königsberg](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsberg "Königsberg") in Prussia (now [Kaliningrad](/wiki/Kaliningrad "Kaliningrad"), Russia); and Memel in Prussia (now [Klaipėda](/wiki/Klaip%C4%97da "Klaipėda"), Lithuania). Wheat production and trade brought Prussia into a close relationship with the [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") during the period of time from 1356 (official founding of the Hanseatic League) until the decline of the League in about 1500.

The expansion of Prussia based on its connection with the [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") cut both [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") off from the coast of the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") and trade abroad.[[6]](#cite_note-7) This meant that Poland and Lithuania would be traditional enemies of Prussia, which was still called the Teutonic Knights.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Duchy of Prussia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Prussia "Duchy of Prussia"), [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia"), and [Free State of Prussia](/wiki/Free_State_of_Prussia "Free State of Prussia")

### Teutonic Order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Teutonic Order")]

Main article: [Monastic state of the Teutonic Knights](/wiki/Monastic_state_of_the_Teutonic_Knights "Monastic state of the Teutonic Knights")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Ordensstaat-kirchlich.jpg/330px-Ordensstaat-kirchlich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ordensstaat-kirchlich.jpg)

Situation after the conquest in the late 13th century. Areas in purple under control of the Monastic State of the Teutonic Knights.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Teutonic_state_1466.png/330px-Teutonic_state_1466.png)](/wiki/File:Teutonic_state_1466.png)

The Teutonic Order (orange) following the [Second Peace of Thorn (1466)](/wiki/Second_Peace_of_Thorn_(1466) "Second Peace of Thorn (1466)")

In 1211, King [Andrew II of Hungary](/wiki/Andrew_II_of_Hungary "Andrew II of Hungary") granted [Burzenland](/wiki/Burzenland "Burzenland") in [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania") as a [fiefdom](/wiki/Fiefdom "Fiefdom") to the [Teutonic Knights](/wiki/Teutonic_Knights "Teutonic Knights"), a German [military order](/wiki/Military_order_(society) "Military order (society)") of [crusading](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades") knights, headquartered in the [Kingdom of Jerusalem](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem") at [Acre](/wiki/Acre,_Israel "Acre, Israel"). In 1225, he expelled them, and they transferred their operations to the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") area. [Konrad I](/wiki/Konrad_I_of_Masovia "Konrad I of Masovia"), the Polish [Duke of Masovia](/wiki/Duke_of_Masovia "Duke of Masovia"), had unsuccessfully attempted to conquer [pagan Prussia](/wiki/Prussia_(region) "Prussia (region)") in [crusades](/wiki/Northern_Crusades "Northern Crusades") in 1219 and 1222.[[8]](#cite_note-9) In 1226, Duke Konrad invited the Teutonic Knights to conquer the Baltic [Prussian tribes](/wiki/Old_Prussians "Old Prussians") on his borders.

During 60 years of [struggles against the Old Prussians](/wiki/Prussian_uprisings "Prussian uprisings"), the Order established an independent state that came to control Prūsa. After the [Livonian Brothers of the Sword](/wiki/Livonian_Brothers_of_the_Sword "Livonian Brothers of the Sword") joined the Teutonic Order in 1237, the Order also controlled [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia "Livonia") (now [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia") and [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia")). Around 1252 they finished the conquest of the northernmost Prussian tribe of the [Skalvians](/wiki/Skalvians "Skalvians") as well as of the western Baltic [Curonians](/wiki/Curonians "Curonians"), and erected [Memel Castle](/wiki/Memel_Castle "Memel Castle"), which developed into the major port city of [Memel](/wiki/Klaip%C4%97da "Klaipėda"). The [Treaty of Melno](/wiki/Treaty_of_Melno "Treaty of Melno") defined the final border between Prussia and the adjoining [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania") in 1422.

The [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") officially formed in northern Europe in 1356 as a group of trading cities. This League came to hold a monopoly on all trade leaving the interior of Europe and Scandinavia and on all sailing trade in the Baltic Sea for foreign countries.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

In the course of the *[Ostsiedlung](/wiki/Ostsiedlung "Ostsiedlung")* (German eastward expansion) process, settlers were invited by German-speaking episcopal and secular authorities, bringing changes in the ethnic composition as well as in language, culture, and law of the eastern borders of the German lands. As a majority of these settlers were Germans, [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German") became the dominant language.

The Knights of the Teutonic Order were subordinate to the [papacy](/wiki/Papacy "Papacy") and to the [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor"). Their initially close relationship with the Polish Crown deteriorated after they conquered Polish-controlled [Pomerelia](/wiki/Pomerelia "Pomerelia") and [Danzig](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk") in 1308. Eventually, Poland and Lithuania, allied through the [Union of Krewo](/wiki/Union_of_Krewo "Union of Krewo") (1385), defeated the Knights in the [Battle of Grunwald](/wiki/Battle_of_Grunwald "Battle of Grunwald") (Tannenberg) in 1410.

The [Thirteen Years' War](/wiki/Thirteen_Years%27_War_(1454%E2%80%9366) "Thirteen Years' War (1454–66)") (1454–1466) began when the [Prussian Confederation](/wiki/Prussian_Confederation "Prussian Confederation"), a coalition of Hanseatic cities of western Prussia, rebelled against the Order and requested help from the Polish king, [Casimir IV Jagiellon](/wiki/Casimir_IV_Jagiellon "Casimir IV Jagiellon"). The Teutonic Knights were forced to acknowledge the sovereignty of, and to pay tribute to Casimir IV in the [Second Peace of Thorn (1466)](/wiki/Second_Peace_of_Thorn_(1466) "Second Peace of Thorn (1466)"), losing western Prussia ([Royal Prussia](/wiki/Royal_Prussia "Royal Prussia")) to Poland in the process. Pursuant to the Second Peace of Thorn, two Prussian states were established.[[10]](#cite_note-11)[*[need quotation to verify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

During the period of the monastic state of the Teutonic Knights, mercenaries from the Holy Roman Empire were granted lands by the Order and gradually formed a new landed Prussian nobility, from which the [Junkers](/wiki/Junker_(Prussia) "Junker (Prussia)") would evolve to take a major role in the militarization of Prussia and, later, Germany.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

### Duchy of Prussia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Duchy of Prussia")]

Main articles: [Prussian Homage](/wiki/Prussian_Homage "Prussian Homage"), [Duchy of Prussia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Prussia "Duchy of Prussia"), and [Crown of the Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Prussian_Homage.jpg/330px-Prussian_Homage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prussian_Homage.jpg)

*[Prussian Homage](/wiki/Prussian_Homage_(painting) "Prussian Homage (painting)")* by [Jan Matejko](/wiki/Jan_Matejko "Jan Matejko"). After admitting the dependence of Prussia to the [Polish Crown](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland"), [Albert of Prussia](/wiki/Albert_of_Prussia "Albert of Prussia") receives [Ducal Prussia](/wiki/Ducal_Prussia "Ducal Prussia") as a [fief](/wiki/Fief "Fief") from King [Sigismund I the Old](/wiki/Sigismund_I_the_Old "Sigismund I the Old") of [Poland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Poland_(1385%E2%80%931569) "Kingdom of Poland (1385–1569)") in 1525.

On 10 April 1525, after signing of the [Treaty of Kraków](/wiki/Treaty_of_Krak%C3%B3w "Treaty of Kraków"), which officially ended the [Polish–Teutonic War (1519–21)](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Teutonic_War_(1519%E2%80%9321) "Polish–Teutonic War (1519–21)"), in the [main square](/wiki/Main_Square,_Krak%C3%B3w "Main Square, Kraków") of the Polish capital [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków"), [Albert I](/wiki/Albert,_Duke_of_Prussia "Albert, Duke of Prussia") resigned his position as [Grand Master of the Teutonic Order](/wiki/Grand_Master_of_the_Teutonic_Order "Grand Master of the Teutonic Order") and received the title "Duke of Prussia" from King [Zygmunt I the Old](/wiki/Zygmunt_I_the_Old "Zygmunt I the Old") of Poland. As a symbol of vassalage, Albert received a standard with the Prussian coat of arms from the Polish king. The black Prussian eagle on the flag was augmented with a letter "S" (for Sigismundus) and had a crown placed around its neck as a symbol of submission to Poland. Albert I, a member of a cadet branch of the [House of Hohenzollern](/wiki/House_of_Hohenzollern "House of Hohenzollern") became a [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") Protestant and secularized the Order's Prussian territories.[[12]](#cite_note-13) This was the area east of the mouth of the [Vistula](/wiki/Vistula "Vistula") river, later sometimes called "Prussia proper". For the first time, these lands came into the hands of a branch of the Hohenzollern family, who already ruled the [Margraviate of Brandenburg](/wiki/Margraviate_of_Brandenburg "Margraviate of Brandenburg"), since the 15th century. Furthermore, with his renunciation of the Order, Albert could now marry and produce legitimate heirs.

### Brandenburg-Prussia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Brandenburg-Prussia")]

Main articles: [Brandenburg-Prussia](/wiki/Brandenburg-Prussia "Brandenburg-Prussia") and [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire")

Brandenburg and Prussia united two generations later. In 1594 [Duchess Anna of Prussia](/wiki/Duchess_Anna_of_Prussia "Duchess Anna of Prussia"), granddaughter of Albert I and daughter of [Albert Frederick, Duke of Prussia](/wiki/Albert_Frederick,_Duke_of_Prussia "Albert Frederick, Duke of Prussia") (reigned 1568–1618), married her cousin [Elector](/wiki/Prince-Elector "Prince-Elector") [John Sigismund](/wiki/John_Sigismund,_Margrave_of_Brandenburg "John Sigismund, Margrave of Brandenburg") of Brandenburg. When Albert Frederick died in 1618 without male heirs, John Sigismund was granted the right of succession to the Duchy of Prussia, then still a Polish fief. From this time the Duchy of Prussia was in [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") with the Margraviate of Brandenburg. The resulting state, known as [Brandenburg-Prussia](/wiki/Brandenburg-Prussia "Brandenburg-Prussia"), consisted of geographically disconnected territories in Prussia, Brandenburg, and the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland") lands of [Cleves](/wiki/Duchy_of_Cleves "Duchy of Cleves") and [Mark](/wiki/County_of_Mark "County of Mark").

During the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War") (1618–1648), various armies repeatedly marched across the disconnected Hohenzollern lands, especially the occupying [Swedes](/wiki/Swedish_Empire "Swedish Empire"). The ineffective and militarily weak Elector [George William](/wiki/George_William,_Elector_of_Brandenburg "George William, Elector of Brandenburg") (1619–1640) fled from Berlin to [Königsberg](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsberg "Königsberg"), the historic capital of the Duchy of Prussia, in 1637. His successor, [Frederick William I](/wiki/Frederick_William,_the_Great_Elector "Frederick William, the Great Elector") (1640–1688), reformed the [army](/wiki/Prussian_Army "Prussian Army") to defend the lands.

Frederick William I went to [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") in 1641 to render [homage](/wiki/Homage_(feudal) "Homage (feudal)") to King [Władysław IV Vasa](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_IV_Vasa "Władysław IV Vasa") of Poland for the Duchy of Prussia, which was still held in [fief](/wiki/Fief "Fief") from the Polish crown. In January 1656, during the first phase of the [Second Northern War](/wiki/Second_Northern_War "Second Northern War") (1654–1660), he received the duchy [as a fief from the Swedish king](/wiki/Treaty_of_K%C3%B6nigsberg_(1656) "Treaty of Königsberg (1656)") who later granted him full sovereignty in the [Treaty of Labiau](/wiki/Treaty_of_Labiau "Treaty of Labiau") (November 1656). In 1657 the Polish king renewed this grant in the treaties of [Wehlau](/wiki/Treaty_of_Wehlau "Treaty of Wehlau") and [Bromberg](/wiki/Treaty_of_Bromberg "Treaty of Bromberg"). With Prussia, the Brandenburg [Hohenzollern dynasty](/wiki/House_of_Hohenzollern "House of Hohenzollern") now held a territory free of any feudal obligations, which constituted the basis for their later elevation to kings.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Nason%2C_Pieter_-_Kurf%C3%BCrst_Friedrich_Wilhelm_und_seine_Gemahlin_Luise_Henriette_-_1666_-_SPSG_2.jpg/250px-Nason%2C_Pieter_-_Kurf%C3%BCrst_Friedrich_Wilhelm_und_seine_Gemahlin_Luise_Henriette_-_1666_-_SPSG_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nason,_Pieter_-_Kurf%C3%BCrst_Friedrich_Wilhelm_und_seine_Gemahlin_Luise_Henriette_-_1666_-_SPSG_2.jpg)

The "[Great Elector](/wiki/Frederick_William_I,_Elector_of_Brandenburg "Frederick William I, Elector of Brandenburg")" and [his wife](/wiki/Luise_Henriette_of_Nassau "Luise Henriette of Nassau")

Frederick William I succeeded in organizing the electorate by establishing an [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") in Brandenburg-Prussia, an achievement for which he became known as the "Great Elector". Above all, he emphasised the importance of a powerful military to protect the state's disconnected territories, while the [Edict of Potsdam](/wiki/Edict_of_Potsdam "Edict of Potsdam") (1685) opened Brandenburg-Prussia for the immigration of Protestant refugees (especially [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot")), and he established a bureaucracy to carry out state administration efficiently.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

### Kingdom of Prussia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Kingdom of Prussia")]

Main article: [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Frederick_I_of_Prussia.jpg/250px-Frederick_I_of_Prussia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frederick_I_of_Prussia.jpg)

[Frederick I](/wiki/Frederick_I_of_Prussia "Frederick I of Prussia"), [King in Prussia](/wiki/King_in_Prussia "King in Prussia")

On 18 January 1701, Frederick William's son, Elector Frederick III, elevated Prussia from a duchy to a kingdom and crowned himself King [Frederick I](/wiki/Frederick_I_of_Prussia "Frederick I of Prussia"). In the [Crown Treaty](/wiki/Crown_Treaty "Crown Treaty") of 16 November 1700, [Leopold I](/wiki/Leopold_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Leopold I, Holy Roman Emperor"), emperor of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), allowed Frederick only to title himself "[King in Prussia](/wiki/King_in_Prussia "King in Prussia")", not "[King of Prussia](/wiki/King_of_Prussia "King of Prussia")". The state of Brandenburg-Prussia became commonly known as "Prussia", although most of its territory, in Brandenburg, Pomerania, and western Germany, lay outside Prussia proper. The Prussian state grew in splendour during the reign of Frederick I, who sponsored the arts at the expense of the treasury.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

Frederick I was succeeded by his son, [Frederick William I](/wiki/Frederick_William_I_of_Prussia "Frederick William I of Prussia") (1713–1740), the austere "Soldier King", who did not care for the arts but was thrifty and practical.[[15]](#cite_note-16) He was the main creator of the vaunted Prussian bureaucracy and the professionalised standing army, which he developed into one of the most powerful in Europe. His troops only briefly [saw action](/wiki/Siege_of_Stralsund_(1711%E2%80%931715) "Siege of Stralsund (1711–1715)") during the [Great Northern War](/wiki/Great_Northern_War "Great Northern War"). In view of the size of the army in relation to the total population, [Mirabeau](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Boniface_Louis_Riqueti_de_Mirabeau "André Boniface Louis Riqueti de Mirabeau"), among others, has been attributed with coining the saying "Prussia, is not a state with an army, but an army with a state."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Frederick William also settled more than 20,000 Protestant refugees from [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg") in thinly populated East Prussia, which was eventually extended to the west bank of the [Neman](/wiki/Neman "Neman") river, and other regions. In the [Treaty of Stockholm](/wiki/Treaty_of_Stockholm_(Great_Northern_War) "Treaty of Stockholm (Great Northern War)") (1720), he acquired half of [Swedish Pomerania](/wiki/Swedish_Pomerania "Swedish Pomerania").[[16]](#cite_note-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Friedrich_Wilhelm_I_1713.jpg/250px-Friedrich_Wilhelm_I_1713.jpg)](/wiki/File:Friedrich_Wilhelm_I_1713.jpg)

King [Frederick William I](/wiki/Frederick_William_I_of_Prussia "Frederick William I of Prussia"), "the Soldier-King"

Frederick William I died in 1740 and was succeeded by his son, [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_II_of_Prussia "Frederick II of Prussia"), whose accomplishments led to his reputation as "Frederick the Great".[[17]](#cite_note-18) As crown prince, Frederick had focused, primarily, on philosophy and the arts.[[18]](#cite_note-19) He was an accomplished flute player and composer. In 1740, Prussian troops crossed over the undefended border of [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia") and rapidly conquered the region. Silesia was the richest province of [Habsburg Austria](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy").[[19]](#cite_note-20) It signalled the beginning of three [Silesian Wars](/wiki/Silesian_Wars "Silesian Wars") (1740–1763).[[20]](#cite_note-21) The [First Silesian War](/wiki/First_Silesian_War "First Silesian War") (1740–1742) and the [Second Silesian War](/wiki/Second_Silesian_War "Second Silesian War") (1744–1745) have, historically, been grouped together with the general European war called the [War of the Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Austrian_Succession "War of the Austrian Succession") (1740–1748). [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") [Charles VI](/wiki/Charles_VI,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles VI, Holy Roman Emperor") had died on 20 October 1740. He was succeeded to the throne by his daughter, [Maria Theresa](/wiki/Maria_Theresa "Maria Theresa").

By defeating the Austrian Army at the [Battle of Mollwitz](/wiki/Battle_of_Mollwitz "Battle of Mollwitz") on 10 April 1741, Frederick succeeded in conquering [Lower Silesia](/wiki/Lower_Silesia "Lower Silesia") (the northwestern half of Silesia).[[21]](#cite_note-22) In the next year, 1742, he conquered [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia") (the southeastern half). Furthermore, in the [Third Silesian War](/wiki/Third_Silesian_War "Third Silesian War") (part of the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War")) Frederick won a victory over Austria at the [Battle of Lobositz](/wiki/Battle_of_Lobositz "Battle of Lobositz") on 1 October 1756. In spite of some victories afterward, his situation became far less comfortable the following years, as he failed in his attempts to knock Austria out of the war and was gradually reduced to a desperate defensive war. However, he never gave up and on 3 November 1760 the Prussian king won another battle, the hard-fought [Battle of Torgau](/wiki/Battle_of_Torgau "Battle of Torgau"). Despite being several times on the verge of defeat Frederick, [allied with](/wiki/Anglo-Prussian_alliance_(1756) "Anglo-Prussian alliance (1756)") [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"), [Hanover](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hanover "Electorate of Hanover") and [Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Landgraviate_of_Hesse-Kassel "Landgraviate of Hesse-Kassel"), was finally able to hold the whole of Silesia against a coalition of [Saxony](/wiki/Electorate_of_Saxony "Electorate of Saxony"), the Habsburg monarchy, [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") and [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire").[[22]](#cite_note-23) [Voltaire](/wiki/Voltaire "Voltaire"), a close friend of the king, once described Frederick the Great's Prussia by saying "...it was [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta") in the morning, [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") in the afternoon."

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Friedrich_der_Gro%C3%9Fe_-_Johann_Georg_Ziesenis_-_Google_Cultural_Institute_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Friedrich_der_Gro%C3%9Fe_-_Johann_Georg_Ziesenis_-_Google_Cultural_Institute_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Friedrich_der_Gro%C3%9Fe_-_Johann_Georg_Ziesenis_-_Google_Cultural_Institute_(cropped).jpg)

King [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_the_Great "Frederick the Great"), "the Great"

Silesia, full of rich soils and prosperous manufacturing towns, became a vital region to Prussia, greatly increasing the nation's area, population, and wealth.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Success on the battleground against Austria and other powers proved Prussia's status as one of the [great powers](/wiki/Great_power "Great power") of Europe. The Silesian Wars began more than a century of rivalry and conflict between Prussia and Austria as the two most powerful states operating within the Holy Roman Empire (although both had extensive territory outside the empire).[[24]](#cite_note-25) In 1744, the [County of East Frisia](/wiki/County_of_East_Frisia "County of East Frisia") fell to Prussia following the extinction of its ruling [Cirksena](/wiki/Cirksena "Cirksena") dynasty.

In the last 23 years of his reign until 1786, Frederick II, who understood himself as the "first servant of the state", promoted the development of Prussian areas such as the [Oderbruch](/wiki/Oderbruch "Oderbruch"). At the same time he built up Prussia's military power and participated in the [First Partition of Poland](/wiki/First_Partition_of_Poland "First Partition of Poland") with Austria and Russia in 1772, an act that geographically connected the Brandenburg territories with those of Prussia proper. The partition also added Polish [Royal Prussia](/wiki/Royal_Prussia "Royal Prussia") to the kingdom, allowing Frederick to re-style himself King *of* Prussia. During this period, he also opened Prussia's borders to immigrants fleeing from religious persecution in other parts of Europe, such as the [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots"). Prussia became a safe haven in much the same way that the United States welcomed immigrants seeking freedom in the 19th century.[[25]](#cite_note-Clark,_Iron_Kingdom_ch_7-26)

Frederick the Great (reigned 1740–1786) practised [enlightened absolutism](/wiki/Enlightened_absolutism "Enlightened absolutism"). He built the world's best army, and usually won his many wars. He introduced a general civil code, abolished torture and established the principle that the Crown would not interfere in matters of justice.[[26]](#cite_note-27) He also promoted an advanced secondary education, the forerunner of today's German [gymnasium](/wiki/Gymnasium_(school) "Gymnasium (school)") (grammar school) system, which prepares the brightest pupils for university studies. The [Prussian education system](/wiki/Prussian_education_system "Prussian education system") was emulated in various countries, including the United States.[[25]](#cite_note-Clark,_Iron_Kingdom_ch_7-26)

#### Napoleonic Wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Napoleonic Wars")]

Main articles: [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), [Battle of Jena-Auerstedt](/wiki/Battle_of_Jena-Auerstedt "Battle of Jena-Auerstedt"), and [War of the Sixth Coalition § War in Germany](/wiki/War_of_the_Sixth_Coalition#War_in_Germany "War of the Sixth Coalition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Acprussiamap2.gif/500px-Acprussiamap2.gif)](/wiki/File:Acprussiamap2.gif)

Growth of [Brandenburg-Prussia](/wiki/Brandenburg-Prussia "Brandenburg-Prussia"), 1600–1795

During the reign of King [Frederick William II](/wiki/Frederick_William_II_of_Prussia "Frederick William II of Prussia") (1786–1797), Prussia annexed additional Polish territory through the [Second Partition of Poland](/wiki/Second_Partition_of_Poland "Second Partition of Poland") in 1793 and the [Third Partition of Poland](/wiki/Third_Partition_of_Poland "Third Partition of Poland") in 1795. His successor, [Frederick William III](/wiki/Frederick_William_III_of_Prussia "Frederick William III of Prussia") (1797–1840), announced the union of the Prussian [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and [Reformed churches](/wiki/Reformed_churches "Reformed churches") into [one church](/wiki/Prussian_Union_of_Churches "Prussian Union of Churches").[[27]](#cite_note-Clark,_Iron_Kingdom_ch_12-28)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Friedrich_Wilhelm_III.%2C_K%C3%B6nig_von_Preu%C3%9Fen_%28unbekannter_Maler%29.jpg/250px-Friedrich_Wilhelm_III.%2C_K%C3%B6nig_von_Preu%C3%9Fen_%28unbekannter_Maler%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Friedrich_Wilhelm_III.,_K%C3%B6nig_von_Preu%C3%9Fen_(unbekannter_Maler).jpg)

King [Frederick William III](/wiki/Frederick_William_III_of_Prussia "Frederick William III of Prussia")

Prussia took a leading part in the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars"), but remained quiet for more than a decade because of the [Peace of Basel](/wiki/Peace_of_Basel "Peace of Basel") of 1795, only to go once more to war with France in 1806 as negotiations with that country over the allocation of the spheres of influence in Germany failed. Prussia suffered a devastating defeat against [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon")'s troops in the [Battle of Jena-Auerstedt](/wiki/Battle_of_Jena-Auerstedt "Battle of Jena-Auerstedt"), leading Frederick William III and his family to flee temporarily to [Memel](/wiki/Klaip%C4%97da "Klaipėda"). Under the [Treaties of Tilsit](/wiki/Treaties_of_Tilsit "Treaties of Tilsit") in 1807, the state lost about one-third of its area, including the areas gained from the second and third [Partitions of Poland](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland"), which now fell to the [Duchy of Warsaw](/wiki/Duchy_of_Warsaw "Duchy of Warsaw"). Beyond that, the king was obliged to pay a large indemnity, to cap his army at 42,000 men, and to let the French garrison troops throughout Prussia, effectively making the kingdom a French satellite.[[28]](#cite_note-Clark,_Iron_Kingdom_ch_11-29)

In response to this defeat, reformers such as [Stein](/wiki/Heinrich_Friedrich_Karl_Reichsfreiherr_vom_und_zum_Stein "Heinrich Friedrich Karl Reichsfreiherr vom und zum Stein") and [Hardenberg](/wiki/Karl_August_von_Hardenberg "Karl August von Hardenberg") set about [modernising the Prussian state.](/wiki/Prussian_Reform_Movement "Prussian Reform Movement") Among their reforms were the liberation of peasants from [serfdom](/wiki/Serfdom "Serfdom"), the [Emancipation of Jews](/wiki/Jewish_Emancipation "Jewish Emancipation") and making full citizens of them. The school system was rearranged, and in 1818 free trade was introduced. The process of army reform ended in 1813 with the introduction of compulsory military service for men.[[29]](#cite_note-30) By 1813, Prussia could mobilize almost 300,000 soldiers, more than half of which were conscripts of the *[Landwehr](/wiki/Landwehr "Landwehr")* of variable quality. The rest consisted of regular soldiers that were deemed excellent by most observers, and very determined to repair the humiliation of 1806.

After the [defeat of Napoleon in Russia](/wiki/Napoleon%27s_invasion_of_Russia "Napoleon's invasion of Russia"), Prussia quit its alliance with France and took part in the [Sixth Coalition](/wiki/Sixth_Coalition "Sixth Coalition") during the "Wars of Liberation" (*Befreiungskriege*) against the French occupation. Prussian troops under Marshal [Gebhard Leberecht von Blücher](/wiki/Gebhard_Leberecht_von_Bl%C3%BCcher "Gebhard Leberecht von Blücher") contributed crucially (alongside the British and Dutch) to the final victory over Napoleon at the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo") of June 1815. Prussia's reward in 1815 at the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") was the recovery of her lost territories, as well as the whole of the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland"), [Westphalia](/wiki/Westphalia "Westphalia"), 40% of [Saxony](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Saxony "Kingdom of Saxony") and some other territories. These western lands were of vital importance because they included the [Ruhr](/wiki/Ruhr "Ruhr") region, the centre of Germany's fledgling industrialisation, especially in the arms industry. These territorial gains also meant the doubling of Prussia's population. In exchange, Prussia withdrew from areas of central Poland to allow the creation of [Congress Poland](/wiki/Congress_Poland "Congress Poland") under Russian sovereignty.[[28]](#cite_note-Clark,_Iron_Kingdom_ch_11-29) In 1815 Prussia became part of the [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation").

#### Wars of liberation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Wars of liberation")]

Main article: [German revolutions of 1848–1849](/wiki/German_revolutions_of_1848%E2%80%931849 "German revolutions of 1848–1849")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Friedrich_Wilhelm_IV_of_Prussia_%281847%29.jpg/250px-Friedrich_Wilhelm_IV_of_Prussia_%281847%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Friedrich_Wilhelm_IV_of_Prussia_(1847).jpg)

King [Frederick William IV](/wiki/Frederick_William_IV_of_Prussia "Frederick William IV of Prussia")

The first half of the 19th century saw a prolonged struggle in Germany between liberals, who wanted a united, federal Germany under a democratic constitution, and [conservatives](/wiki/Conservatism "Conservatism"), who wanted to maintain Germany as a patchwork of independent, monarchical states with Prussia and [Austria](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") competing for influence. One small movement that signalled a desire for German unification in this period was the [Burschenschaft](/wiki/Burschenschaft "Burschenschaft") student movement, by students who encouraged the use of the black-red-gold flag, discussions of a unified German nation, and a progressive, liberal political system. Because of Prussia's size and economic importance, smaller states began to join its free trade area in the 1820s. Prussia benefited greatly from the creation in 1834 of the German Customs Union ([Zollverein](/wiki/Zollverein "Zollverein")), which included most German states but excluded Austria.[[27]](#cite_note-Clark,_Iron_Kingdom_ch_12-28)

In 1848, the liberals saw an opportunity when [revolutions broke out across Europe](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848"). Alarmed, King [Frederick William IV](/wiki/Frederick_William_IV_of_Prussia "Frederick William IV of Prussia") agreed to convene a [National Assembly](/wiki/Prussian_National_Assembly "Prussian National Assembly") and grant a [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Prussia_(1848) "Constitution of Prussia (1848)"). When the [Frankfurt Parliament](/wiki/Frankfurt_Parliament "Frankfurt Parliament") offered Frederick William the crown of a united Germany, he refused on the grounds that he would not accept a crown from a revolutionary assembly without the sanction of Germany's other monarchs.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

The Frankfurt Parliament was forced to dissolve in 1849, and Frederick William issued a [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Prussia_(1850) "Constitution of Prussia (1850)") by his own authority in 1850. This conservative document provided for a two-house parliament, the [Landtag of Prussia](/wiki/Landtag_of_Prussia "Landtag of Prussia"). The lower house, or *[Prussian House of Representatives](/wiki/Prussian_House_of_Representatives "Prussian House of Representatives")* was elected by all males over the age of 25. They were divided into [three classes](/wiki/Prussian_three-class_franchise "Prussian three-class franchise") whose votes were weighted according to the amount of taxes paid. In one typical election, the first class (with those who paid the most in taxes) included 4% of voters and the third class (with those who paid the least) had 82%, yet each group chose the same number of electors.[[31]](#cite_note-32) The system but assured dominance by the more well-to-do men of the population. The upper house, the *[Prussian House of Lords](/wiki/Prussian_House_of_Lords "Prussian House of Lords")*, was appointed by the king. He retained full executive authority, and ministers were responsible only to him. As a result, the grip of the landowning classes, the Junkers, remained unbroken, especially in the eastern provinces.[[32]](#cite_note-33) The constitution nevertheless contained a number of liberal elements such as the introduction of jury courts and a catalog of fundamental rights that included freedom of religion, speech and the press.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

#### Wars of unification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Wars of unification")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-2005-0057%2C_Otto_von_Bismarck.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-2005-0057%2C_Otto_von_Bismarck.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-2005-0057,_Otto_von_Bismarck.jpg)

[Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck")

In 1862, King [Wilhelm I](/wiki/Wilhelm_I,_German_Emperor "Wilhelm I, German Emperor") appointed [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") as [Minister President of Prussia](/wiki/Minister_President_of_Prussia "Minister President of Prussia"). Bismarck was determined to defeat both the liberals and conservatives and increase Prussian supremacy and influence among the German states. There has been much debate as to whether Bismarck actually planned to create a united Germany when he set out on this journey, or whether he simply took advantage of the circumstances that fell into place. Bismarck curried support from large sections of the people by promising to lead the fight for greater German unification. He successfully guided Prussia through three wars, which unified Germany and brought William the position of [German Emperor](/wiki/German_Emperor "German Emperor").[[34]](#cite_note-35)

##### Schleswig Wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Schleswig Wars")]

The Kingdom of [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") was at the time in personal union with the Duchies of [Schleswig](/wiki/Duchy_of_Schleswig "Duchy of Schleswig") and [Holstein](/wiki/Duchy_of_Holstein "Duchy of Holstein"), both of which had close ties with each other, although only Holstein was part of the [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation"). When the Danish government tried to integrate Schleswig, but not Holstein, into the Danish state, Prussia led the German Confederation against Denmark in the [First War of Schleswig](/wiki/First_War_of_Schleswig "First War of Schleswig") (1848–1851). Because Russia supported Austria, Prussia also conceded predominance in the German Confederation to Austria in the [Punctation of Olmütz](/wiki/Punctation_of_Olm%C3%BCtz "Punctation of Olmütz") in 1850, resulting in a return to the status quo.

In 1863, Denmark introduced a shared constitution for Denmark and Schleswig. This led to conflict with the German Confederation, which authorised the occupation of Holstein by the Confederation, from which Danish forces withdrew. In 1864, Prussian and Austrian forces crossed the border between Holstein and Schleswig initiating the [Second War of Schleswig](/wiki/Second_War_of_Schleswig "Second War of Schleswig"). The Austro-Prussian forces defeated the Danes, who surrendered both territories. In the resulting [Gastein Convention](/wiki/Gastein_Convention "Gastein Convention") of 1865 Prussia took over the administration of Schleswig while Austria assumed that of Holstein.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

##### Austro-Prussian War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Austro-Prussian War")]

Main article: [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Ac.prussiamap3.png/500px-Ac.prussiamap3.png)](/wiki/File:Ac.prussiamap3.png)

Expansion of Prussia, 1807–1871

Bismarck realised that the dual administration of Schleswig and Holstein was only a temporary solution, and tensions rose between Prussia and Austria. The struggle for supremacy in Germany then led to the [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War") (1866), triggered by the dispute over Schleswig and Holstein, with Bismarck using proposed injustices as the [reason for war](/wiki/Casus_belli "Casus belli").

On the Austrian side stood the south German states (including [Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria") and [Württemberg](/wiki/Kingdom_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Kingdom of Württemberg")), some central German states (including [Saxony](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Saxony "Kingdom of Saxony")), and [Hanover](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hanover "Kingdom of Hanover") in the north. On the side of Prussia were [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"), most north German states, and some smaller central German states. Eventually, the better-armed Prussian troops won the crucial victory at the [Battle of Königgrätz](/wiki/Battle_of_K%C3%B6niggr%C3%A4tz "Battle of Königgrätz") under [Helmuth von Moltke the Elder](/wiki/Helmuth_von_Moltke_the_Elder "Helmuth von Moltke the Elder"). The century-long struggle between Berlin and Vienna for the dominance of Germany was now over. As a sideshow in this war, Prussia defeated Hanover in the [Battle of Langensalza (1866)](/wiki/Battle_of_Langensalza_(1866) "Battle of Langensalza (1866)"). While Hanover hoped in vain for help from Britain (as they had previously been in personal union), Britain stayed out of a confrontation with a continental great power and Prussia satisfied its desire for merging the once separate territories and gaining strong economic and strategic power, particularly from the full access to the resources of the Ruhr.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

Bismarck desired Austria as an ally in the future, and so he declined to annex any Austrian territory. But in the [Peace of Prague](/wiki/Peace_of_Prague_(1866) "Peace of Prague (1866)") in 1866, Prussia annexed four of Austria's allies in northern and central Germany – Hanover, [Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hesse "Electorate of Hesse"), [Nassau](/wiki/Duchy_of_Nassau "Duchy of Nassau") and [Frankfurt](/wiki/Free_City_of_Frankfurt "Free City of Frankfurt"). Prussia also won full control of [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein"). As a result of these territorial gains, Prussia now stretched uninterrupted across the northern two-thirds of Germany and contained two-thirds of Germany's population. The German Confederation was dissolved, and Prussia impelled the 21 states north of the [Main](/wiki/Main_(river) "Main (river)") river into forming the [North German Confederation](/wiki/North_German_Confederation "North German Confederation").

Prussia was the dominant state in the new confederation, as the kingdom comprised almost four-fifths of the new state's territory and population. Prussia's near-total control over the confederation was secured in the [constitution](/wiki/North_German_Constitution "North German Constitution") drafted for it by Bismarck in 1867. Executive power was held by a [president](/wiki/Bundespr%C3%A4sidium "Bundespräsidium"), assisted by a chancellor responsible only to him. The presidency was a hereditary office of the [Hohenzollern](/wiki/House_of_Hohenzollern "House of Hohenzollern") rulers of Prussia. There was also a two-house parliament. The lower house, or *[Reichstag](/wiki/Reichstag_(North_German_Confederation) "Reichstag (North German Confederation)")* (Diet), was elected by universal male [suffrage](/wiki/Suffrage "Suffrage"). The upper house, or *[Bundesrat](/wiki/Bundesrat_(German_Empire) "Bundesrat (German Empire)")* (Federal Council) was appointed by the state governments. The Bundesrat was, in practice, the stronger chamber. Prussia had 17 of 43 votes, and could easily control proceedings through alliances with the other states.

As a result of the peace negotiations, the states south of the Main remained theoretically independent, but received the (compulsory) protection of Prussia. Additionally, mutual defence treaties were concluded. However, the existence of these treaties was kept secret until Bismarck made them public in 1867 when France tried to [acquire Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_Crisis "Luxembourg Crisis").

##### Franco-Prussian War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Franco-Prussian War")]

Main article: [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Kaiser_Wilhelm_I._.JPG/250px-Kaiser_Wilhelm_I._.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kaiser_Wilhelm_I._.JPG)

Kaiser [Wilhelm I](/wiki/William_I,_German_Emperor "William I, German Emperor")

The controversy with the [Second French Empire](/wiki/Second_French_Empire "Second French Empire") over the candidacy of [Leopold, Prince of Hohenzollern](/wiki/Leopold,_Prince_of_Hohenzollern "Leopold, Prince of Hohenzollern") to the Spanish throne was escalated both by France and Bismarck. With his [Ems Dispatch](/wiki/Ems_Dispatch "Ems Dispatch"), Bismarck took advantage of an incident in which the French ambassador had approached William. The government of [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III"), expecting another civil war among the German states, declared war against Prussia, continuing [Franco-German enmity](/wiki/Franco-German_enmity "Franco-German enmity"). However, honouring their treaties, the German states joined forces and quickly defeated France in the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") in 1870. Following victory under Bismarck's and Prussia's leadership, [Baden](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Baden "Grand Duchy of Baden"), Württemberg and Bavaria, which had remained outside the North German Confederation, accepted incorporation into a united [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire").

The empire was a "Lesser German" solution (in German, "[kleindeutsche Lösung](/wiki/Kleindeutsche_L%C3%B6sung "Kleindeutsche Lösung")") to the question of uniting all German-speaking peoples into one state, because it excluded Austria, which remained connected to [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary") and whose territories included non-German populations. On 18 January 1871 (the 170th anniversary of the coronation of King [Frederick I](/wiki/Frederick_I_of_Prussia "Frederick I of Prussia")), William was [proclaimed](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_German_Empire "Proclamation of the German Empire") "German [Emperor](/wiki/Kaiser "Kaiser")" (not "Emperor of Germany") in the [Hall of Mirrors](/wiki/Hall_of_Mirrors "Hall of Mirrors") at [Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles") outside Paris, while [the French capital was still under siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Paris_(1870%E2%80%931871) "Siege of Paris (1870–1871)").

#### German Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: German Empire")]

Main article: [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Prussiamap.gif/500px-Prussiamap.gif)](/wiki/File:Prussiamap.gif)

Prussia in the German Empire from 1871 to 1918

The two decades after the [unification of Germany](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany") were the peak of Prussia's fortunes, but the seeds for potential strife were built into the Prusso-German political system.

The [Constitution of the German Empire](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_German_Empire "Constitution of the German Empire") was a version of the North German Confederation's constitution. Officially, the German Empire was a federal state. In practice, Prussia overshadowed the rest of the empire. Prussia included three-fifths of the German territory and two-thirds of its population. The [Imperial German Army](/wiki/Imperial_German_Army "Imperial German Army") was, in practice, an enlarged Prussian army, although the other kingdoms ([Bavaria](/wiki/Bavarian_Army "Bavarian Army"), [Saxony](/wiki/Royal_Saxon_Army "Royal Saxon Army") and [Württemberg](/wiki/Army_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Army of Württemberg")) retained their own small armies, coming under Imperial control in wartime. The imperial crown was a hereditary office of the House of Hohenzollern, the royal house of Prussia. The Minister President of Prussia was, except for two brief periods (January–November 1873 and 1892–94), also [imperial chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany"). But the empire itself had no right to collect taxes directly from its subjects; the only incomes fully under federal control were the customs duties, common [excise](/wiki/Excise "Excise") duties, and the revenue from postal and telegraph services. While all men above age 25 were eligible to vote in imperial elections, Prussia retained its restrictive three-class voting system. This effectively required the king/emperor and prime minister/chancellor to seek majorities from legislatures elected by two different franchises. In both the kingdom and the empire, the original constituencies were never redrawn to reflect changes in population, meaning that rural areas were grossly overrepresented by the turn of the 20th century.

As a result, Prussia and the German Empire were something of a paradox. Bismarck knew that his new German Empire was now a colossus and economically and militarily dominant in Europe; Britain was still dominant in finance, trade and at sea. He declared Germany a "satisfied" power, using his talents to preserve peace, for example at the [Congress of Berlin](/wiki/Congress_of_Berlin "Congress of Berlin"). Bismarck did not set up his own party. He had mixed success in some of his domestic policies. His anti-Catholic *[Kulturkampf](/wiki/Kulturkampf "Kulturkampf")* inside Prussia (and not the wider German state) was a failure. He ended his support for the anticlerical [Liberals](/wiki/National_Liberal_Party_(Germany) "National Liberal Party (Germany)") and worked instead with the Catholic [Centre Party](/wiki/Centre_Party_(Germany) "Centre Party (Germany)"). He tried to destroy the socialist movement, with limited success. The large Polish population resisted [Germanisation](/wiki/Germanisation_of_Poles_during_Partitions "Germanisation of Poles during Partitions").[[37]](#cite_note-38)

[Frederick III](/wiki/Frederick_III,_German_Emperor "Frederick III, German Emperor") became emperor in March 1888, after the death of his father, but he died of cancer only 99 days later.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Kaiser_Wilhelm_II_of_Germany_-_1902.jpg/250px-Kaiser_Wilhelm_II_of_Germany_-_1902.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kaiser_Wilhelm_II_of_Germany_-_1902.jpg)

[Kaiser Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II,_German_Emperor "Wilhelm II, German Emperor")

At age 29, Wilhelm became Kaiser [Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II,_German_Emperor "Wilhelm II, German Emperor") after a difficult youth and conflicts with his British mother [Victoria, Princess Royal](/wiki/Victoria,_Princess_Royal "Victoria, Princess Royal"). He turned out to be a man of limited experience, narrow and reactionary views, poor judgment, and occasional bad temper, which alienated former friends and allies.

### Railways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Railways")]

Main article: [Prussian state railways](/wiki/Prussian_state_railways "Prussian state railways")

Prussia nationalised its railways in the 1880s in an effort both to lower rates on freight service and to equalise those rates among shippers. Instead of lowering rates as far as possible, the government ran the railways as a profit-making endeavour, and the railway profits became a major source of revenue for the state. The nationalisation of the railways slowed the economic development of Prussia because the state favoured the relatively backward agricultural areas in its railway building. Moreover, the railway surpluses substituted for the development of an adequate tax system.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

### The Free State of Prussia in the Weimar Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: The Free State of Prussia in the Weimar Republic")]

Main article: [Free State of Prussia](/wiki/Free_State_of_Prussia "Free State of Prussia")

Because of the [German Revolution](/wiki/German_Revolution "German Revolution") of 1918, Wilhelm II abdicated as German Emperor and King of Prussia. Prussia was proclaimed a "Free State" (i.e. a republic, German: *Freistaat*) within the new [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic") and in 1920 received a democratic [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Prussia_(1920) "Constitution of Prussia (1920)").

Almost all of Germany's territorial losses, specified in the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles"), were areas that had been part of Prussia: [Eupen](/wiki/Eupen "Eupen") and [Malmedy](/wiki/Malmedy "Malmedy") to [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"); [North Schleswig](/wiki/South_Jutland_County "South Jutland County") to Denmark; the [Memel Territory](/wiki/Klaip%C4%97da_Region "Klaipėda Region") to [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"); the [Hultschin area](/wiki/Hlu%C4%8D%C3%ADnsko "Hlučínsko") to [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"). Many of the areas Prussia annexed in the [partitions of Poland](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland"), such as the provinces of [Posen](/wiki/Province_of_Posen "Province of Posen") and [West Prussia](/wiki/West_Prussia "West Prussia"), as well as eastern [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia"), went to the [Second Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic"). [Danzig](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk") became the [Free City of Danzig](/wiki/Free_City_of_Danzig "Free City of Danzig") under the administration of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations"). Also, the [Saargebiet](/wiki/Saargebiet "Saargebiet") was created mainly from formerly Prussian territories, except present [Saarpfalz](/wiki/Saarpfalz-Kreis "Saarpfalz-Kreis") district, which was part of the Kingdom of Bavaria. [East Prussia](/wiki/East_Prussia "East Prussia") became an exclave, only reachable by ship. (the [Sea Service East Prussia](/wiki/Seedienst_Ostpreu%C3%9Fen "Seedienst Ostpreußen")) or by a railway through the [Polish corridor](/wiki/Polish_corridor "Polish corridor").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Weimar_Republic_states_map.svg/500px-Weimar_Republic_states_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Weimar_Republic_states_map.svg)

Federal states of the Weimar Republic, with Prussia in light gray. After [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") the provinces of [Posen](/wiki/Province_of_Posen "Province of Posen") and [West Prussia](/wiki/West_Prussia_Province "West Prussia Province") came largely to the [2nd Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic"); [Posen-West Prussia](/wiki/Posen-West_Prussia "Posen-West Prussia") and the [West Prussia district](/wiki/Regierungsbezirk_Westpreu%C3%9Fen "Regierungsbezirk Westpreußen") were formed from the remaining parts.

The German government seriously considered breaking up Prussia into smaller states, but eventually traditionalist sentiment prevailed and Prussia became by far the largest state of the Weimar Republic, comprising 60% of its territory. With the abolition of the older Prussian franchise, it became a stronghold of the left. Its incorporation of "Red Berlin" and the industrialised Ruhr Area, both with working-class majorities, ensured left-wing dominance.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

From 1919 to 1932, Prussia was governed by a coalition of the [Social Democrats](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany"), [Catholic Centre](/wiki/Centre_Party_(Germany) "Centre Party (Germany)") and [German Democrats](/wiki/German_Democratic_Party "German Democratic Party"); from 1921 to 1925, coalition governments included the [German People's Party](/wiki/German_People%27s_Party "German People's Party"). Unlike in other states of the German Reich, majority rule by democratic parties in Prussia was never endangered. Nevertheless, in East Prussia and some rural areas, the [Nazi Party](/wiki/National_Socialist_German_Workers_Party "National Socialist German Workers Party") of [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") gained more and more influence and popular support, especially from the lower middle class starting in 1930. Except for Catholic [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia"), the Nazi Party in 1932 became the largest party in most parts of the Free State of Prussia. However, the democratic parties in coalition remained a majority, while [Communists](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Germany "Communist Party of Germany") and Nazis were in the opposition.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

The East Prussian [Otto Braun](/wiki/Otto_Braun "Otto Braun"), who was Prussian minister-president almost continuously from 1920 to 1932, is considered one of the most capable Social Democrats in history. He implemented several trend-setting reforms together with his minister of the interior, [Carl Severing](/wiki/Carl_Severing "Carl Severing"), which were also models for the later [Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). For instance, a Prussian minister-president could be forced out of office only if there was a "positive majority" for a potential successor. This concept, known as the [constructive vote of no confidence](/wiki/Constructive_vote_of_no_confidence "Constructive vote of no confidence"), was carried over into the [Basic Law for the Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/Basic_Law_for_the_Federal_Republic_of_Germany "Basic Law for the Federal Republic of Germany"). Most historians regard the Prussian government during this time as far more successful than that of Germany as a whole.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

In contrast to its pre-war authoritarianism, Prussia was a pillar of democracy in the Weimar Republic. This system was destroyed by the *[Preußenschlag](/wiki/Preu%C3%9Fenschlag "Preußenschlag")* ("Prussian coup") of Reich Chancellor [Franz von Papen](/wiki/Franz_von_Papen "Franz von Papen"). In this [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état"), the government of the Reich deposed the Prussian government on 20 July 1932, under the pretext that the latter had lost control of public order in Prussia (during the [Bloody Sunday](/wiki/Altona_Bloody_Sunday "Altona Bloody Sunday") of [Altona, Hamburg](/wiki/Altona,_Hamburg "Altona, Hamburg"), which was still part of Prussia at that time) and by using fabricated evidence that the Social Democrats and the Communists were planning a joint "putsch". The Defence Minister General [Kurt von Schleicher](/wiki/Kurt_von_Schleicher "Kurt von Schleicher"), who was the prime mover behind the coup, manufactured evidence that the Prussian police under Braun's orders were favouring the Communist *[Rotfrontkämpferbund](/wiki/Rotfrontk%C3%A4mpferbund "Rotfrontkämpferbund")* in street clashes with the [SA](/wiki/Sturmabteilung "Sturmabteilung") as part of an alleged plan to foment a Marxist revolution, which he used to get an emergency decree from President [Paul von Hindenburg](/wiki/Paul_von_Hindenburg "Paul von Hindenburg") imposing *Reich* control on Prussia.[[42]](#cite_note-43) Papen appointed himself Reich commissioner for Prussia and took control of the government. The *Preußenschlag* made it easier, only half a year later, for Hitler to take power decisively in Germany, since he had the whole apparatus of the Prussian government, including the police, at his disposal.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

### Prussia and Nazi Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Prussia and Nazi Germany")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Hitler_portrait_crop.jpg/250px-Hitler_portrait_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hitler_portrait_crop.jpg)

[Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Former_eastern_territories_of_Germany.png/250px-Former_eastern_territories_of_Germany.png)](/wiki/File:Former_eastern_territories_of_Germany.png)

Territory lost after World War I

Territory lost after World War II

Present-day Germany

After the appointment of Hitler as the new chancellor, the Nazis used the absence of Franz von Papen as an opportunity to appoint [Hermann Göring](/wiki/Hermann_G%C3%B6ring "Hermann Göring") federal commissioner for the Prussian ministry of the interior. The [Reichstag election](/wiki/March_1933_German_federal_election "March 1933 German federal election") of 5 March 1933 strengthened the position of the National Socialist German Workers' Party ([NSDAP](/wiki/NSDAP "NSDAP") or "Nazi" Party), although they did not achieve an absolute majority.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

The [Reichstag building](/wiki/Reichstag_building "Reichstag building") having been [set on fire](/wiki/Reichstag_fire "Reichstag fire") a few weeks earlier on 27 February, a new [Reichstag](/wiki/Reichstag_(Weimar_Republic) "Reichstag (Weimar Republic)") was opened in the [Garrison Church](/wiki/Garrison_Church_(Potsdam) "Garrison Church (Potsdam)") of [Potsdam](/wiki/Potsdam "Potsdam") on 21 March 1933 in the presence of President Paul von Hindenburg. In a propaganda-filled meeting between Hitler and the Nazi Party, the "marriage of old Prussia with young Germany" was celebrated, to win over the Prussian monarchists, conservatives and nationalists and induce them into supporting and subsequently voting in favor of the [Enabling Act of 1933](/wiki/Enabling_Act_of_1933 "Enabling Act of 1933").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-C06886%2C_Paul_v._Hindenburg.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-C06886%2C_Paul_v._Hindenburg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-C06886,_Paul_v._Hindenburg.jpg)

[Paul von Hindenburg](/wiki/Paul_von_Hindenburg "Paul von Hindenburg")

In the centralised state created by the Nazis in the "[Law on the Reconstruction of the Reich](/wiki/Law_on_the_Reconstruction_of_the_Reich "Law on the Reconstruction of the Reich")" ("Gesetz über den Neuaufbau des Reichs", 30 January 1934) and the "[Law on Reich Governors](/wiki/Reichsstatthalter#Reich_Governors_Law_(1935) "Reichsstatthalter")" ("Reichsstatthaltergesetz", 30 January 1935) the states were dissolved, in fact if not in law. The state *Landtage* were abolished and the state governments were now controlled by *[Reichsstatthaltern](/wiki/Reichsstatthalter "Reichsstatthalter")* (Reich Governors) who were appointed by the chancellor. Parallel to that, the organisation of the party into districts (*[Gaue](/wiki/Gau_(German) "Gau (German)")*) gained increasing importance, as the official in charge of a *Gau* (the head of which was called a *[Gauleiter](/wiki/Gauleiter "Gauleiter")*) was again appointed by the chancellor who was at the same time chief of the Nazi Party.

This centralising policy went even further in Prussia. From 1934 to 1945, almost all ministries were merged and only a few departments were able to maintain their independence. [Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") himself became formally the governor of Prussia. However, his functions were exercised by Hermann Göring as Prussian prime minister.

As provided for in the "[Greater Hamburg Act](/wiki/Greater_Hamburg_Act "Greater Hamburg Act")" ("Groß-Hamburg-Gesetz"), certain exchanges of territory took place. Prussia was extended on 1 April 1937, for instance, by the incorporation of the Free and Hanseatic City of [Lübeck](/wiki/L%C3%BCbeck "Lübeck").

The Prussian lands transferred to Poland after the Treaty of Versailles were re-annexed during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). However, most of this territory was not reintegrated back into Prussia but assigned to separate *Gaue* of [Danzig-West Prussia](/wiki/Danzig-West_Prussia "Danzig-West Prussia") and [Wartheland](/wiki/Wartheland "Wartheland") during much of the duration of the war.

### The end of Prussia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: The end of Prussia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Germany_former_prussian_lander.png/250px-Germany_former_prussian_lander.png)](/wiki/File:Germany_former_prussian_lander.png)

Map of the current [states of Germany](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") (in dark green) that are completely or mostly situated inside the old borders of [Imperial Germany](/wiki/Imperial_Germany "Imperial Germany")'s [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia")

The areas east of the [Oder–Neisse line](/wiki/Oder%E2%80%93Neisse_line "Oder–Neisse line"), mainly Eastern Prussia, Western Prussia, [Pomerania](/wiki/Pomerania "Pomerania") and [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia"), were annexed by Poland and the Soviet Union in 1945 owing to the [Treaty of Potsdam](/wiki/Treaty_of_Potsdam "Treaty of Potsdam") between three of the Allies: the United States, United Kingdom, and the Soviet Union. This included important Prussian cities like [Danzig](/wiki/Danzig "Danzig"), [Königsberg](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsberg "Königsberg"), [Breslau](/wiki/Breslau "Breslau"), and [Stettin](/wiki/Stettin "Stettin"). The [population fled, mostly to the Western zones, or was driven out.](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_(1944%E2%80%9350) "Flight and expulsion of Germans (1944–50)")

As part of their wartime goals, the Western allies sought the [abolition of Prussia](/wiki/Abolition_of_Prussia "Abolition of Prussia"). [Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") was initially content to retain the name, Russians having a different historical view of their neighbour and sometime former ally. Nonetheless, by [Law No. 46](/wiki/Abolition_of_Prussia "Abolition of Prussia"), which was accepted and implemented by the [Allied Control Council](/wiki/Allied_Control_Council "Allied Control Council") on 25 February 1947, Prussia was officially proclaimed to be dissolved.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

In the [Soviet occupation zone](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_zone "Soviet occupation zone"), which became [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") (officially, the German Democratic Republic) in 1949, the former Prussian territories were reorganised into the states of [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg") and [Saxony-Anhalt](/wiki/Saxony-Anhalt "Saxony-Anhalt"), with the remaining parts of the [Province of Pomerania](/wiki/Province_of_Pomerania_(1815%E2%80%931945) "Province of Pomerania (1815–1945)") going to [Mecklenburg-Western Pomerania](/wiki/Mecklenburg-Western_Pomerania "Mecklenburg-Western Pomerania"). These states were *de facto* abolished in 1952 in favour of *[Bezirke](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_East_Germany#Division_into_Bezirke "Administrative divisions of East Germany")* (districts), but were [recreated](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_East_Germany#Restoration_of_the_Länder "Administrative divisions of East Germany") shortly before [German reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification") in 1990.

In the [Western Zones of occupation](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Germany "Allied-occupied Germany"), which became [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") (officially, the Federal Republic of Germany) in 1949, the former Prussian territories were divided up among [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia"), [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony"), [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse"), [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate") and [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein"). [Württemberg-Baden](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Baden "Württemberg-Baden") and [Württemberg-Hohenzollern](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Hohenzollern "Württemberg-Hohenzollern") were later merged with [Baden](/wiki/Baden "Baden") to create the state of [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg"). The Saar region, which had been administered by the French as [a protectorate](/wiki/Saar_Protectorate "Saar Protectorate") separate from the rest of Western Germany, was admitted to the Federal Republic of Germany as [a separate state](/wiki/Saarland "Saarland") following the [1955 Saar Statute referendum](/wiki/1955_Saar_Statute_referendum "1955 Saar Statute referendum").

One year later, in 1957, the [Prussian Cultural Heritage Foundation](/wiki/Prussian_Cultural_Heritage_Foundation "Prussian Cultural Heritage Foundation") was established and implemented by federal statutes in West Germany in response to a ruling from the [Federal Constitutional Court of Germany](/wiki/Federal_Constitutional_Court "Federal Constitutional Court"). The fundamental goal of this institution is protecting the cultural legacy of Prussia. As of 2021, it continues to operate from its headquarters in Berlin.

## Administrative and constitutional frameworks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Administrative and constitutional frameworks")]
