Main articles: [Brandenburg-Prussia](/wiki/Brandenburg-Prussia "Brandenburg-Prussia") and [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia")

In the mid-16th century, the [margraves of Brandenburg](/wiki/List_of_rulers_of_Brandenburg "List of rulers of Brandenburg") had become highly dependent on the estates (representing counts, lords, knights, and towns, but not prelates, owing to the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") in 1538).[[46]](#cite_note-Kotulla262-47) The margraviate's liabilities and tax income as well as the margrave's finances were in the hands of the *Kreditwerk*, an institution not controlled by the elector, and of the *Großer Ausschuß* (Great Committee) of the estates.[[47]](#cite_note-Kotulla263-48) This was because of concessions made by [Elector Joachim II](/wiki/Joachim_II,_Elector_of_Brandenburg "Joachim II, Elector of Brandenburg") in 1541 in return for financial aid by the estates; however, the *Kreditwerk* went bankrupt between 1618 and 1625.[[47]](#cite_note-Kotulla263-48) The margraves further had to yield to the veto of the estates in all issues concerning the "better or worse of the country", in all legal commitments, and in all issues concerning pawn or sale of the elector's real property.[[47]](#cite_note-Kotulla263-48)

[Hohenzollern](/wiki/House_of_Hohenzollern "House of Hohenzollern") [residence in Berlin](/wiki/Stadtschloss,_Berlin "Stadtschloss, Berlin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Berliner_Schloss_um_1690_von_der_langen_Br%C3%BCcke_Abraham_Jansz._Begeyn.jpg/250px-Berliner_Schloss_um_1690_von_der_langen_Br%C3%BCcke_Abraham_Jansz._Begeyn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Berliner_Schloss_um_1690_von_der_langen_Br%C3%BCcke_Abraham_Jansz._Begeyn.jpg)

... during the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Rosenberg_Schloss_1781.jpg/250px-Rosenberg_Schloss_1781.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rosenberg_Schloss_1781.jpg)

... according to the design of 1781

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Berlin_Nationaldenkmal_Kaiser_Wilhelm_mit_Schloss_1900.jpg/250px-Berlin_Nationaldenkmal_Kaiser_Wilhelm_mit_Schloss_1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Berlin_Nationaldenkmal_Kaiser_Wilhelm_mit_Schloss_1900.jpg)

... according to the design of 1900

To reduce the influence of the estates, in 1604, [Joachim Frederick](/wiki/Joachim_Frederick,_Elector_of_Brandenburg "Joachim Frederick, Elector of Brandenburg") created a council called *Geheimer Rat für die Kurmark* (Privy Council for the Electorate), which instead of the estates would function as the supreme advisory council for the elector.[[47]](#cite_note-Kotulla263-48) While the council was permanently established in 1613, it failed to gain any influence until 1651, owing to the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War")[[47]](#cite_note-Kotulla263-48) (1618–1648)

Until after the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War"), the various territories of Brandenburg-Prussia remained politically independent from each other,[[46]](#cite_note-Kotulla262-47) connected only by the common feudal superior.[[48]](#cite_note-Kotulla265-49) [Frederick William](/wiki/Frederick_William_I,_Elector_of_Brandenburg "Frederick William I, Elector of Brandenburg") (ruled 1640–1688), who envisioned the transformation of the [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") into a [real union](/wiki/Real_union "Real union"),[[48]](#cite_note-Kotulla265-49) started to centralise the Brandenburg-Prussian government with an attempt to establish the *Geheimer Rat* as a central authority for all territories in 1651, but this project proved unfeasible.[[49]](#cite_note-Kotulla267-50) Instead, the elector continued to appoint a governor (*Kurfürstlicher Rat*) for each territory, who in most cases was a member of the *Geheimer Rat*.[[49]](#cite_note-Kotulla267-50) The most powerful institution in the territories remained the governments of the estates (*Landständische Regierung*, named *Oberratsstube* in Prussia and *Geheime Landesregierung* in Mark and Cleves), which were the highest government agencies regarding jurisdiction, finances and administration.[[49]](#cite_note-Kotulla267-50) The elector attempted to balance the estates' governments by creating *Amtskammer* chambers to administer and coordinate the elector's domains, tax income and privileges.[[49]](#cite_note-Kotulla267-50) Such chambers were introduced in Brandenburg in 1652, in Cleves and Mark in 1653, in Pomerania in 1654, in Prussia in 1661 and in Magdeburg in 1680.[[49]](#cite_note-Kotulla267-50) Also in 1680, the *Kreditwerk* came under the aegis of the elector.[[50]](#cite_note-Kotulla266-51)

Frederick William I's excise tax (*Akzise*), which from 1667 replaced the property tax raised in Brandenburg for Brandenburg-Prussia's standing army with the estates' consent, was raised by the elector without consultation with the estates.[[50]](#cite_note-Kotulla266-51) The conclusion of the [Second Northern War](/wiki/Second_Northern_War "Second Northern War") of 1655–1660 had strengthened the elector politically, enabling him to reform the constitution of Cleves and Mark in 1660 and 1661 to introduce officials loyal to him and independent of the local estates.[[50]](#cite_note-Kotulla266-51) In the Duchy of Prussia he confirmed the traditional privileges of the [Prussian estates](/wiki/Prussian_estates "Prussian estates") in 1663,[[50]](#cite_note-Kotulla266-51) but the latter accepted the caveat that these privileges were not to be used to interfere with the exertion of the elector's sovereignty.[[49]](#cite_note-Kotulla267-50) As in Brandenburg, Frederick William ignored the privilege of the Prussian estates to confirm or veto taxes raised by the elector: while in 1656, an *Akzise* was raised with the estates' consent, the elector by force collected taxes not approved by the Prussian estates for the first time in 1674.[[49]](#cite_note-Kotulla267-50) From 1704 the Prussian estates *de facto* relinquished their right to approve the elector's taxes while formally still entitled to do so.[[49]](#cite_note-Kotulla267-50) In 1682 the elector introduced an *Akzise* to Pomerania and in 1688 to Magdeburg,[[49]](#cite_note-Kotulla267-50) while in Cleves and Mark an *Akzise* was introduced only between 1716 and 1720. Owing to Frederick William I's reforms, the state income increased threefold during his reign, and the tax burden per subject reached a level twice as high as in France.[[50]](#cite_note-Kotulla266-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Corona_Prusia-mj2.jpg/250px-Corona_Prusia-mj2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Corona_Prusia-mj2.jpg)

[Prussian King's Crown](/wiki/Crown_of_William_II "Crown of William II") ([Hohenzollern Castle](/wiki/Hohenzollern_Castle "Hohenzollern Castle") Collection)

Under the rule of [Frederick III (I)](/wiki/Frederick_I_of_Prussia "Frederick I of Prussia") (reign: 1688–1713), the Brandenburg Prussian territories were *de facto* reduced to provinces of the monarchy.[[48]](#cite_note-Kotulla265-49) Frederick William's testament would have divided Brandenburg-Prussia among his sons, but his firstborn son Frederick III (I), with the [emperor](/wiki/Leopold_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Leopold I, Holy Roman Emperor")'s backing, succeeded in becoming the sole ruler based on the [House Treaty of Gera](/wiki/House_Treaty_of_Gera "House Treaty of Gera") of 1599, which forbade a division of Hohenzollern territories.[[51]](#cite_note-Kotulla269-52) In 1689, a new central chamber for all Brandenburg-Prussian territories was established, called *Geheime Hofkammer* (from 1713: *Generalfinanzdirektorium*). This chamber functioned as a superior agency of the territories' *Amtskammer* chambers.[[52]](#cite_note-Kotulla270-53) The [General War Commissariat](/wiki/General_War_Commissariat "General War Commissariat") (*Generalkriegskommissariat*) emerged as a second central agency, superior to the local *Kriegskommissariat* agencies initially concerned with the administration of the army, but before 1712 transformed into an agency also concerned with general tax and police tasks.[[52]](#cite_note-Kotulla270-53)

The Kingdom of Prussia functioned as an [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") until the [German revolutions of 1848–1849](/wiki/German_revolutions_of_1848%E2%80%931849 "German revolutions of 1848–1849"), after which Prussia became a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") and King [Frederick William IV](/wiki/Frederick_William_IV "Frederick William IV") appointed [Adolf Heinrich von Arnim-Boitzenburg](/wiki/Adolf_Heinrich_von_Arnim-Boitzenburg "Adolf Heinrich von Arnim-Boitzenburg") Prussia's first [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Prussia "Prime Minister of Prussia") (*Ministerpräsident*).[[53]](#cite_note-54) [Prussia's first constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Prussia_(1848) "Constitution of Prussia (1848)") dated from 1848, but was only briefly in effect as it had been forced on the king. The [1850 Prussian Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Prussia_(1850) "Constitution of Prussia (1850)") established a [two-chamber](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") parliament. The lower house, the [Prussian House of Representatives](/wiki/Prussian_House_of_Representatives "Prussian House of Representatives") represented all taxpayers, who were [divided into three classes](/wiki/Prussian_three-class_franchise "Prussian three-class franchise") according to the amount of taxes paid. This assured dominance by the more well-to-do elements of the population. The upper house (First Chamber or *Erste Kammer*), later renamed the [Prussian House of Lords](/wiki/Prussian_House_of_Lords "Prussian House of Lords") (*Herrenhaus*), was appointed by the king. He retained full executive authority and ministers were responsible only to him. As a result, the grip of the landowning classes, the Junkers, remained unbroken, especially in the eastern provinces. The [Prussian Secret Police](/wiki/Prussian_Secret_Police "Prussian Secret Police"), formed in response to the German revolutions of 1848–1849, aided the conservative government.

### Prussia inside Weimar Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Prussia inside Weimar Republic")]

Main article: [Free State of Prussia](/wiki/Free_State_of_Prussia "Free State of Prussia")

Unlike its authoritarian pre-1918 predecessor, Prussia from 1918 to 1932 was a promising democracy within Germany. The abolition of the political power of the aristocracy transformed Prussia into a region strongly dominated by the left wing of the political spectrum, with "Red Berlin" and the industrial centre of the [Ruhr Area](/wiki/Ruhr_Area "Ruhr Area") exerting major influence. During this period a coalition of centre-left parties ruled, predominantly under the leadership (1920–1932) of East Prussian [Social Democrat](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") [Otto Braun](/wiki/Otto_Braun "Otto Braun"). While in office Braun implemented several reforms (together with his Minister of the Interior, [Carl Severing](/wiki/Carl_Severing "Carl Severing")) that became models for the later Federal Republic of Germany. For instance, a Prussian prime minister could only be forced out of office if there was a "positive majority" for a potential successor. This concept, known as the [constructive vote of no confidence](/wiki/Constructive_vote_of_no_confidence "Constructive vote of no confidence"), became part of the Basic Law of the Federal Republic of Germany. Historians regard the Prussian government during the 1920s as far more successful than that of Germany as a whole.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

Similar to other German states both [now](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") and [at the time](/wiki/Weimar_Republic#Constituent_states "Weimar Republic"), executive power remained vested in a [Minister-President of Prussia](/wiki/Minister-President "Minister-President") and in laws established by a [Landtag](/wiki/Landtag "Landtag") elected by the people.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Curonians_kursenieki_in_1649.png/250px-Curonians_kursenieki_in_1649.png)](/wiki/File:Curonians_kursenieki_in_1649.png)

In 1649, [Kursenieki](/wiki/Kursenieki "Kursenieki") settlements along the [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") coastline of [East Prussia](/wiki/East_Prussia "East Prussia") spanned from [Memel (Klaipėda)](/wiki/Klaip%C4%97da "Klaipėda") to [Danzig (Gdańsk)](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk").

## Social history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Social history")]

### Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Population")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Ethnic_structure_of_eastern_regions_of_Prussia.png/250px-Ethnic_structure_of_eastern_regions_of_Prussia.png)](/wiki/File:Ethnic_structure_of_eastern_regions_of_Prussia.png)

Ethnic structure of the eastern regions of Prussia between 1817 and 1823

In 1871, Prussia's population numbered 24.69 million, accounting for 60% of the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire")'s population.[[55]](#cite_note-Handbuch-56) The population grew rapidly from 45 million in 1880 to 56 million in 1900, thanks to declining mortality, even as birth rates declined. About 6 million Germans, primarily young families, migrated to the United States, especially the mid-western farming regions. Their place in agriculture was often taken by young Polish farm workers. In addition, large numbers of Polish miners moved to Upper Silesia and many Germans and Poles moved to industrial jobs in the fast-growing cities especially in the Rhineland and Westphalia.[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58) In 1910, the population had increased to 40.17 million (62% of the Empire's population).[[55]](#cite_note-Handbuch-56) In 1914, Prussia had an area of 354,490 km2. In May 1939, Prussia had an area of 297,007 km2 and a population of 41,915,040 inhabitants.

### Ethnicity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Ethnicity")]

Apart from ethnic Germans the country was inhabited also by [ethnolinguistic](/wiki/Ethnolinguistic_group "Ethnolinguistic group") minorities such as [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") (including [Kashubs](/wiki/Kashubians "Kashubians") in West Prussia and [Mazurs](/wiki/Masurians "Masurians") in East Prussia), [Prussian Lithuanians](/wiki/Prussian_Lithuanians "Prussian Lithuanians") (in East Prussia), [Sorbs](/wiki/Sorbs "Sorbs") (in Lusatia), [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs") and [Moravians](/wiki/Moravians "Moravians") (in Silesia), [Danes](/wiki/Danes "Danes") (in Schleswig), [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews"), [Frisians](/wiki/Frisians "Frisians"), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people"), [Walloons](/wiki/Walloons "Walloons"), Russians (in [Wojnowo](/wiki/Wojnowo,_Warmian-Masurian_Voivodeship "Wojnowo, Warmian-Masurian Voivodeship")), [French](/wiki/French_people "French people"), [Italians](/wiki/Italians "Italians"), [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians") and others.[[58]](#cite_note-:02-59)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Religion")]

Further information: [Prussian Union of Churches](/wiki/Prussian_Union_of_Churches "Prussian Union of Churches"), [Old Lutherans](/wiki/Old_Lutherans "Old Lutherans"), and [Kulturkampf](/wiki/Kulturkampf "Kulturkampf")

The [Duchy of Prussia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Prussia "Duchy of Prussia") was the first state to [officially adopt](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") Lutheranism in 1525. In the wake of the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), Prussia was dominated by two major [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") confessions: [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism"). The majority of the Prussian population was Lutheran, although there were dispersed [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist") minorities in central and western parts of the state especially [Brandenburg](/wiki/Province_of_Brandenburg "Province of Brandenburg"), [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhine_Province "Rhine Province"), [Westphalia](/wiki/Province_of_Westphalia "Province of Westphalia") and [Hesse-Nassau](/wiki/Province_of_Hesse-Nassau "Province of Hesse-Nassau"). In 1613, [John Sigismund, Elector of Brandenburg and Grand Duke of Prussia](/wiki/John_Sigismund,_Elector_of_Brandenburg "John Sigismund, Elector of Brandenburg") declared himself for the Calvinist creed and transferred the [Berlin Cathedral](/wiki/Berlin_Cathedral "Berlin Cathedral") from the Lutheran to the Calvinist church. Lutherans and Calvinist congregations all over the kingdom were merged in 1817 by the [Prussian Union of churches](/wiki/Prussian_Union_of_churches "Prussian Union of churches"), which came under tight royal control.[[59]](#cite_note-60) In Protestant regions, writes Nipperdey:

> Much of religious life was often conventional and superficial by any normal, human standard. The state and the bureaucracy kept their distance, preferring to spoon-feed the churches and treat them like children. They saw the churches as channels for education, as a means of instilling morality and obedience, or for propagating useful things, just like bee-keeping or potato-farming.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

Prussia received significant [Huguenot](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot") population after the issuing of the [Edict of Fontainebleau](/wiki/Edict_of_Fontainebleau "Edict of Fontainebleau") by [Louis XIV of France](/wiki/Louis_XIV_of_France "Louis XIV of France") and the following [dragonnades](/wiki/Dragonnades "Dragonnades"). Prussian monarchs, beginning with [Frederick William, Elector of Brandenburg](/wiki/Frederick_William,_the_Great_Elector "Frederick William, the Great Elector"), opened the country to the fleeing French Calvinist refugees. In Berlin, they built and worshipped at their own church called the [French Cathedral](/wiki/French_Cathedral,_Berlin "French Cathedral, Berlin") on [Gendarmenmarkt](/wiki/Gendarmenmarkt "Gendarmenmarkt"). Time passed by, and the French Reformed assimilated into the wider Protestant community in Prussia. East Prussia's southern region of [Masuria](/wiki/Masuria "Masuria") was mostly made up of [Germanised](/wiki/Germanisation "Germanisation") [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") [Masurians](/wiki/Masurians "Masurians").

After 1814, Prussia contained millions of Catholics in the west and in the east. There were substantial populations in the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland"), parts of [Westphalia](/wiki/Westphalia "Westphalia"), eastern parts of [Silesia](/wiki/Province_of_Silesia "Province of Silesia"), [West Prussia](/wiki/West_Prussia "West Prussia"), [Ermland](/wiki/Ermland "Ermland") and the [Province of Posen](/wiki/Province_of_Posen "Province of Posen").[[61]](#cite_note-62) Communities in Poland were often ethnically [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people"), although this is not the case of eastern Silesia as the majority of Catholics there were German. During the 19th-century [Kulturkampf](/wiki/Kulturkampf "Kulturkampf"), Prussian Catholics were forbidden from fulfilling any official functions for the state and were largely distrusted.

Prussia contained a relatively large Jewish community, which was mostly concentrated in large urban areas. According to the 1880 census, it was the biggest one in Germany with 363,790 individuals.

In 1925, 64.9% of the Prussian population was Protestant, 31.3% was Catholic, 1.1% was Jewish, 2.7% was placed in other religious categories.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

### Non-German population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Non-German population")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Polish_language_in_1910.png/250px-Polish_language_in_1910.png)](/wiki/File:Polish_language_in_1910.png)

Polish language distribution (light blue) in 1910

In 1871, approximately 2.4 million Poles lived in Prussia, constituting the largest minority.[[55]](#cite_note-Handbuch-56) Other minorities were Jews, [Danes](/wiki/Danes "Danes"), [Frisians](/wiki/Frisians "Frisians"), [Dutchmen](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people"), [Kashubians](/wiki/Kashubians "Kashubians") (72,500 in 1905), [Masurians](/wiki/Masurians "Masurians") (248,000 in 1905), [Lithuanians](/wiki/Lithuanians "Lithuanians") (101,500 in 1905), [Walloons](/wiki/Walloons "Walloons"), [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs"), [Kursenieki](/wiki/Kursenieki "Kursenieki"), and [Sorbs](/wiki/Sorbs "Sorbs").[[55]](#cite_note-Handbuch-56)

The area of [Greater Poland](/wiki/Greater_Poland "Greater Poland"), where the Polish nation had originated, became the [Province of Posen](/wiki/Province_of_Posen "Province of Posen") after the [Partitions of Poland](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland"). Poles in this Polish-majority province (62% Polish, 38% German) resisted German rule. Also, the southeast portion of Silesia ([Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia")) had a Polish majority. But Catholics and Jews did not have equal status with Protestants.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

As a result of the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") in 1919, the [Second Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic") was granted not only these two areas, but also areas with a German majority in the province of West Prussia. After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), East Prussia, most of Pomerania and Silesia, and the eastern part of Brandenburg were either annexed by the Soviet Union or given to Poland, and the [German-speaking](/wiki/German_language "German language") populations [forcibly expelled](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_from_Poland_during_and_after_World_War_II "Flight and expulsion of Germans from Poland during and after World War II").

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Prussian education system](/wiki/Prussian_education_system "Prussian education system")

The German states in the 19th century were world leaders in prestigious education and Prussia set the pace.[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66) For boys free public education was widely available, and the gymnasium system for elite students was highly professionalized. The modern university system emerged from the 19th century German universities, especially Friedrich Wilhelm University (now named [Humboldt University of Berlin](/wiki/Humboldt_University_of_Berlin "Humboldt University of Berlin")). It pioneered the model of the research university with well-defined career tracks for professors.[[66]](#cite_note-67) The United States, for example, paid close attention to German models. Families focused on educating their sons. The traditional schooling for girls was generally provided by mothers and governesses. Elite families increasingly favoured Catholic convent boarding schools for their daughters. Prussia's Kulturkampf laws in the 1870s limited Catholic schools thus providing an opening for a large number of new private schools for girls.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

## Continuation of Prussian traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Continuation of Prussian traditions")]

The [German states](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") on the former territory of the Free State of Prussia are successor states to Prussia in legal terms, particularly in terms of constitutional and [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law"). For example, the state of [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia") is bound to the [concordat](/wiki/Concordat "Concordat") that the Free State of Prussia concluded with the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See").[[68]](#cite_note-69)[[69]](#cite_note-70)

Despite its dissolution in 1947, many aspects of Prussia have been preserved to this day in everyday life, in [culture](/wiki/Culture "Culture"), in [sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport") and even in names.

### Federal government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Federal government")]

* In the federal government according to the prevailing view, the [Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") as a subject of international law is identical to the federal state initiated and dominated by Prussia, which was founded in 1867 under the name of the [North German Confederation](/wiki/North_German_Confederation "North German Confederation") and expanded into the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") in 1871.
* Prussia's capital [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") also became the capital of the newly founded empire in 1871. The capital city resolution of 1991, which designated Berlin as the federal capital of the reunified Germany, the "Berlin Republic", stands in this tradition. Several federal institutions use buildings from former Prussian institutions, for example the [Bundesrat](/wiki/Federal_Council_(Germany) "Federal Council (Germany)") uses the Prussian House of Lords building. The Federal President has his first official residence in Bellevue Palace, the first classical building in Prussia.[[70]](#cite_note-71) As the central shield of the Reich coat of arms, the Prussian state coat of arms is depicted in the gable above the main entrance to the [Reichstag building](/wiki/Reichstag_building "Reichstag building").
* The [constructive vote of no confidence](/wiki/Constructive_vote_of_no_confidence "Constructive vote of no confidence") anchored in the [Basic Law of the Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/Basic_Law_for_the_Federal_Republic_of_Germany "Basic Law for the Federal Republic of Germany"), which only allows the head of government to be voted out if a new successor is simultaneously elected, is directly based on a constitutional regulation of the Free State of Prussia.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* The Prussian war award of the [Iron Cross](/wiki/Iron_Cross "Iron Cross") is, in a modified form, the symbol of the [Bundeswehr](/wiki/Bundeswehr "Bundeswehr").
* In the tradition of the 1st Guards Regiment on Foot, which was introduced in 1806 as the personal regiment of the King of Prussia, the guard battalion at the Federal Ministry of Defense has followed.
* As part of [state visits](/wiki/State_visit "State visit"), the presentation march of Friedrich Wilhelm III is a regular part of the federal diplomatic protocol at the reception with military honors and the marching down the front of the [guard battalion](/wiki/Wachbataillon "Wachbataillon")'s honor formation at the [Federal Ministry of Defense](/wiki/Federal_Ministry_of_Defence_(Germany) "Federal Ministry of Defence (Germany)") is played.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]
* The Bundeswehr's [Great Tattoo](/wiki/Gro%C3%9Fer_Zapfenstreich "Großer Zapfenstreich"), played particularly when bidding farewell to [Federal Presidents](/wiki/President_of_Germany "President of Germany"), [Chancellors](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany"), Federal Defense Ministers and senior military officers, is largely composed of traditional elements of Prussian military music.
* The police star, the emblem of the [Federal Police](/wiki/Federal_Police_(Germany) "Federal Police (Germany)") and the [Feldjäger](/wiki/Feldj%C3%A4ger "Feldjäger") of the Bundeswehr, is derived from the Prussian Guard Star, which went back to the eight-pointed breast star of the [Black Eagle Order](/wiki/Order_of_the_Black_Eagle "Order of the Black Eagle"). The guard star can also be found on the bell trees of the Bundeswehr music corps.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* In 2002, the then [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg") Social Minister Alwin Ziel suggested naming the planned new federal state of Berlin-Brandenburg "Prussia".

### Within Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Within Germany")]

* The state coat of arms of [Saxony-Anhalt](/wiki/Saxony-Anhalt "Saxony-Anhalt") shows, among other things the [Prussian eagle](/wiki/Prussian_eagle "Prussian eagle").
* The large coat of arms of [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg") contains the house coat of arms of the [Hohenzollerns](/wiki/House_of_Hohenzollern "House of Hohenzollern").
* The Prussian government and administration model was decisive for a large number of political institutions at the state level and is still expressed today in terms such as [Minister-president](/wiki/Minister-president "Minister-president"), [Regierungsbezirk](/wiki/Regierungsbezirk "Regierungsbezirk") and [Landrat](/wiki/Landrat "Landrat"). Today's North Rhine-Westphalia regional associations go back to the Prussian provincial associations.
* The Rhineland Regional Association in North Rhine-Westphalia - in continuation of the tradition of the Rhine Province and its provincial association - also has the Prussian eagle in the upper part of its association coat of arms.
* The states on the former territory of the Free State of Prussia are successor states to Prussia in legal terms, particularly in terms of constitutional and international law. North Rhine-Westphalia, the largest successor state to Prussia,[[72]](#cite_note-73) maintains its Prussian history and remembrance culture in the form of the Prussian Museums in [Wesel](/wiki/Wesel "Wesel") and [Minden](/wiki/Minden "Minden").

### In churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prussia&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: In churches")]

* The [Union of Evangelical Churches](/wiki/Union_of_Protestant_Churches_in_the_EKD "Union of Protestant Churches in the EKD") emerged from the Evangelical Church of the Union, a church association of the Old Prussian Protestant regional churches, i.e. H. of the churches whose area already belonged to Prussia before 1866
* The Prussian state coat of arms is depicted in the niche above the main (west) door within [Berlin Cathedral](/wiki/Berlin_Cathedral "Berlin Cathedral"), relating to the link between church and state.
